---
title: "The course: credible impact evaluation & measurement"
subtitle: "A small, live cohort, built around your own data."
---

Most of what I write here comes back to the same thing: evaluations fall apart at
predictable places, and the fixes aren't fancy. This is the course version of
that. Five weeks, live, in a small group, where you bring your own study (or use
mine) and leave with something defensible.

## Who it's for

People who run or review impact studies in education and want them to hold up:
applied researchers, evaluators, grad students doing quasi experimental work,
ed tech analysts who have to defend an effectiveness claim. If you can run a
regression in R and have a dataset you care about, you're ready. No prior
causal inference coursework assumed.

## What you'll leave with, on your own data

- A defensible evaluation question and identification strategy
- A WWC aligned **baseline equivalence** table (and the reasoning behind each
  covariate's category)
- A **measurement check** on a key construct
- A reproducible script plus a short **memo a decision maker will actually trust**

## The five weeks

1. **From question to design:** estimands, the menu of designs, what each assumes
2. **Baseline equivalence done right:** standardized differences, the WWC
   thresholds, what "satisfied with adjustment" really commits you to (hands on
   with [`baselinr`](https://github.com/zl1212-ship-it/baselinr))
3. **Measurement before models:** reliability and validity, and why a noisy
   outcome quietly kills your effect
4. **Estimation & honest effects:** effect sizes, uncertainty, "significant" vs
   "meaningful"
5. **Communicating credibly + capstone:** turning analysis into a memo, plus your
   own mini evaluation

About 4 to 6 hours a week, including a weekly deliverable. Small cohort, live
sessions (recorded), asynchronous work blocks.

## Instructor

Yuxia Liang. M.Ed. in Educational Leadership & Policy, University of Washington.
I work on impact evaluation, measurement/psychometrics, and computational text
analysis, and I build the open source
[`baselinr`](https://github.com/zl1212-ship-it/baselinr) R package the course uses.

## Join the waitlist

The first cohort and pricing are still being set, and waitlist members hear first
(and get founding cohort terms). Drop your email. No spam, no commitment.

```{=html}
<form action="https://buttondown.com/api/emails/embed-subscribe/esa" method="post" target="_blank" class="embeddable-buttondown-form" style="margin:0.6em 0;">
  <input type="email" name="email" id="bd-email-course" placeholder="you@example.com" required aria-label="Email address" style="padding:0.45em; min-width:18em; margin-right:0.4em;" />
  <input type="submit" value="Join the waitlist" style="padding:0.45em 1em;" />
  <p style="font-size:0.8em; color:#666; margin-top:0.4em;"><a href="https://buttondown.com/refer/esa" target="_blank">Powered by Buttondown.</a></p>
</form>
```
